title: The Art of Minimalist Living
description: Exploring how stripping down the unnecessary reveals what truly matters in everyday life.
content:
Minimalism isn't about owning nothing. It's about owning only what serves you.

For years I accumulated things I didn't need. Books I never read, gadgets that collected dust, clothes that filled closets but rarely saw daylight. One day, I started asking one question before keeping anything: *does this add value to my life?*

The process was uncomfortable at first. We attach identity to objects. Letting go felt like losing pieces of myself. But as I cleared physical clutter, mental fog lifted with it.

## What Minimalism Actually Means

Minimalism is intentionality. It's choosing quality over quantity in every domain: possessions, relationships, commitments, information. When you stop filling space for the sake of it, you make room for what actually matters.

Start small. Pick one drawer. Remove everything that doesn't belong or serve a purpose. Notice how it feels. That clarity is what you're after.

## The Unexpected Benefits

- **Reduced decision fatigue**, fewer choices = more mental energy for what matters
- **Financial freedom**, buying less means spending less
- **Environmental impact**, consumption has a cost beyond your wallet
- **Presence**, uncluttered spaces support uncluttered minds

The journey toward minimalism is deeply personal. There's no right number of possessions. The goal isn't asceticism, it's alignment between what you own and who you are.

Start today. Open one drawer.

title: Why I Switched to a Mechanical Keyboard
description: A deep dive into the satisfying world of clicky keys, custom switches, and the cult of tactile typing.
content:
It started innocuously, a coworker brought in a keyboard that sounded like rainfall on a tin roof.

I asked what it was. Two weeks later, I was on Reddit at 2am reading about spring weights and actuation force. Welcome to the mechanical keyboard rabbit hole.

## The Switch Problem (Literally)

Mechanical keyboards use individual mechanical switches under each key, rather than the rubber domes found in most laptops and cheap keyboards. The three main types:

- **Linear switches** (e.g., Cherry MX Red), smooth from top to bottom, quiet-ish, favored by gamers
- **Tactile switches** (e.g., Brown, Topre), a bump you feel but don't hear when the key registers
- **Clicky switches** (e.g., Blue, Green), tactile bump + audible click. Satisfying. Colleagues will hate you.

I landed on tactile. The feedback loop between finger and keystroke changed how I type, more deliberate, more accurate.

## The Productivity Case

After switching, my typing speed increased by roughly 15 WPM. More importantly, typing *felt* good. When a tool is enjoyable to use, you use it more and better.

There's also the ergonomics angle. Proper actuation force and key travel reduce finger fatigue over long sessions.

## Is It Worth It?

A decent entry-level mechanical keyboard runs $80–150. For someone who types 8+ hours a day, that's not an investment to overthink.

The keycap customization community is a bonus, there's something deeply satisfying about a keyboard that's visually and physically yours.

Just... maybe use headphones in open offices.

title: Learning to Cook From Scratch
description: How teaching myself to cook transformed not just my meals, but my relationship with time, money, and nourishment.
content:
I lived on instant noodles and delivery apps until I was twenty-six.

Not out of laziness, exactly, more like a belief that cooking was a skill you either had or you didn't, and I didn't. Turns out that belief was just wrong.

## The First Dish

I started with scrambled eggs. Sounds trivial, but getting scrambled eggs *right*, low heat, constant movement, off the heat before they look done, teaches patience and attention. Two skills central to all cooking.

From eggs, I moved to pasta. Then soups. Then stir-fries. Each dish taught a technique that transferred to the next.

## What Nobody Tells You

Cooking isn't about recipes, it's about understanding flavor, texture, and heat. Once you understand *why* you sear meat before braising, or why you salt pasta water, you stop following recipes blindly and start improvising.

That shift from follower to creator is when cooking becomes joyful.

## The Ripple Effects

**Financial**: Cooking at home slashed my food spending by more than half.

**Health**: I stopped eating whatever delivery app optimized for my clicks and started eating what I actually wanted.

**Time**: Counterintuitively, meal prepping saved time. Cooking once for three days is more efficient than ordering three times.

**Presence**: Cooking requires you to be here, now. Chopping vegetables doesn't lend itself to doomscrolling.

## Where to Start

Pick one dish you genuinely enjoy eating. Learn to make it. Make it five times until you can do it without looking at a recipe. Then pick another dish.

You'll be cooking in a month. You'll be *cooking* in six.

title: The Case for Reading Physical Books
description: In an age of endless digital content, there's something irreplaceable about ink, paper, and the ritual of reading.
content:
My Kindle sat unused for eight months before I admitted I preferred paper.

The digital evangelists will cite convenience, portability, cost. All valid. But reading is not primarily a logistical exercise. It's a ritual. And the physicality of a book is part of that ritual in ways I kept underestimating.

## The Distraction Problem

When I read on a device, I read *on a device*. The same rectangle that holds Twitter and email notifications holds my books. The pull is constant. A physical book is just a book. There's nowhere else to go.

This single difference has more impact on comprehension and retention than any reading technique.

## Marginalia and Memory

Writing in the margins of physical books is a centuries-old practice for good reason. The act of underlining, starring, and annotating engages the brain differently than passive reading. When I return to a book years later, my own annotations are a conversation across time.

Digital highlights exist, but the friction of exporting and revisiting them is real. My marked-up paperbacks are immediately legible.

## The Tactile Experience

There's genuine pleasure in the weight of a book, the smell of old paper, the resistance of a thick spine. These sensory details signal to the brain that something meaningful is happening. Reading becomes a committed act rather than a casual scroll.

## The Practicalities

Yes, physical books take up space. Yes, they're heavier to travel with. For long trips, the Kindle wins.

But for regular reading at home, for the books I want to remember, annotate, and return to — paper remains unmatched.

Buy the book. Dog-ear the pages if you want to. It's yours.

title: Quiet Productivity: Against Hustle Culture
description: A measured argument for doing less, better — and why the grind aesthetic is selling you something you don't need.
content:
"Rise and grind." "Sleep is for the weak." "If you're not hustling, you're falling behind."

This language is everywhere, and it's doing real damage.

## What Hustle Culture Actually Sells

At its core, hustle culture is an attention product. Influencers monetize anxiety. The message, that you are always behind, always insufficient, always needing to do more, keeps people engaged and spending. It's not productivity advice. It's manufactured urgency.

True productivity looks nothing like the hustle aesthetic.

## The Research Is Clear

Cognitive performance degrades significantly after 8-10 hours of focused work. Sleep deprivation, a badge of honor in hustle culture, impairs judgment, creativity, and emotional regulation. Studies of knowledge workers consistently show that output quality drops after 6 focused hours, even when hours worked increases.

The elite performers in any field — writers, athletes, programmers — almost universally report structured, limited work hours paired with genuine rest.

## What Quiet Productivity Looks Like

- **Deep work first** — protect your best hours for your most important tasks
- **Clear stopping points** — define what "done" looks like before you start
- **Actual rest** — not scrolling; genuine recovery that restores cognitive resources
- **Single-tasking** — context switching destroys efficiency

## The Uncomfortable Truth

Most hustle culture content is produced by people whose business model is *selling hustle culture content*. Your 60-hour week doesn't make their point — it makes their business.

Work with intention. Rest without guilt. Measure your output by what you actually produce, not how many hours you suffered through.

The grind is not the goal. The work is.
